• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Section 3: Logistic Regression

Pablo Arés Gastesi

2026-07-06

1 Introduction

So far we have studied regression: predicting a numeric response from one or more predictors. Now we turn to classification: the response is a category — admitted or rejected, spam or not, diseased or healthy.

Classification is arguably the more common task in practice. A bank decides whether to grant a loan, a doctor decides whether a scan shows a tumour, an email filter decides whether a message is spam. In all cases the question is not how much? but which class?

Logistic regression is the natural starting point. It is conceptually close to linear regression — it uses the same linear combination of predictors — but the output is a probability, which is then turned into a class prediction via a threshold. That threshold is a genuine modelling choice, not a detail, and much of this note is about understanding and evaluating it.

We illustrate everything with a dataset of admissions to Business Schools. The response has three values: admit, border, and notadmit. We start with the binary case (admit vs. not) and finish with the three-class problem.

Further reading: ISLR2 Chapter 4 covers logistic regression in depth.

2 The data

admission <- read_csv("admission.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
colnames(admission)[3] <- "decision"
admission$decision <- factor(admission$decision,
                             levels = c("notadmit", "border", "admit"))

The dataset has 85 applicants described by two numeric scores: GPA (undergraduate grade point average) and GMAT (Graduate Management Admission Test score), plus the admission decision: admit, border, or notadmit.

ggplot(admission, aes(x = GPA, y = GMAT, colour = decision)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(notadmit = "firebrick",
                                 border   = "goldenrod",
                                 admit    = "steelblue")) +
  labs(x = "GPA", y = "GMAT", colour = "Decision") +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 2.1: GPA vs GMAT coloured by admission decision 

admission %>%
  pivot_longer(c(GPA, GMAT), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = decision, y = value, fill = decision)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(notadmit = "firebrick",
                               border   = "goldenrod",
                               admit    = "steelblue")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 2.2: Distribution of GPA and GMAT by decision

Both GPA and GMAT separate the groups reasonably well, though with overlap — especially for the border cases.

2.1 Binary simplification

We begin with the simpler binary problem: admit vs. everything else (merging border and notadmit into a single negative class). We also create a numeric 0/1 coding, which will be useful shortly.

admission <- admission %>%
  mutate(
    decision2 = factor(ifelse(decision == "admit", "admit", "notadmit"),
                       levels = c("notadmit", "admit")),
    y = as.integer(decision2) - 1L   # 0 = notadmit, 1 = admit
  )

3 Why not linear regression?

Since \(y\) is coded as 0/1, one might try a linear model. Let us see what happens when we fit \(y\) on GMAT.

fit_lm <- lm(y ~ GMAT, data = admission)

gmat_grid <- data.frame(GMAT = seq(min(admission$GMAT),
                                   max(admission$GMAT), length.out = 200))
gmat_grid$pred <- predict(fit_lm, newdata = gmat_grid)

ggplot(admission, aes(x = GMAT, y = y)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = decision2), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(data = gmat_grid, aes(y = pred), colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(0, 1), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(notadmit = "firebrick", admit = "steelblue")) +
  labs(x = "GMAT", y = "Predicted value", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 3.1: Linear regression on a 0/1 response: predictions go outside [0,1] 

pred_lm <- predict(fit_lm,
  newdata = data.frame(GMAT = c(min(admission$GMAT), max(admission$GMAT))))
cat("Prediction at GMAT =", min(admission$GMAT), ":", round(pred_lm[1], 3), "\n")
## Prediction at GMAT = 313 : -0.344
cat("Prediction at GMAT =", max(admission$GMAT), ":", round(pred_lm[2], 3), "\n")
## Prediction at GMAT = 693 : 1.191

Both predictions are outside \([0,1]\), which makes no sense as probabilities. The problem is structural: a linear function on \(\mathbb{R}\) cannot be constrained to stay in \([0,1]\).

4 The logistic model

4.1 The sigmoid function

We need a function that maps any real number to the interval \((0,1)\). The standard choice is the logistic (sigmoid) function:

\[\sigma(z) = \frac{e^z}{1 + e^z} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\]

z <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 300)
ggplot(data.frame(z = z, sigma = 1 / (1 + exp(-z))), aes(x = z, y = sigma)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(0, 0.5, 1), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  labs(x = expression(z), y = expression(sigma(z))) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 4.1: The sigmoid function maps any real number to (0, 1)

4.2 The model

The logistic model predicts the probability of \(Y = 1\) given a predictor \(X\) as:

\[p(X) = P(Y = 1 \mid X) = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}}\]

A simple rearrangement gives the log-odds (or logit):

\[\log \frac{p(X)}{1 - p(X)} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X\]

The log-odds are linear in \(X\), even though the probability \(p(X)\) is not. The parameters \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are estimated by maximum likelihood — there is no closed-form solution, unlike OLS.

Interpreting \(\beta_1\): a one-unit increase in \(X\) multiplies the odds by \(e^{\beta_1}\). If \(\beta_1 > 0\), higher \(X\) means higher probability of \(Y = 1\); if \(\beta_1 < 0\), lower.

5 Fitting the model in R

Logistic regression is a special case of a Generalised Linear Model (GLM), fitted with glm(..., family = binomial). The default link is the logit.

fit_gmat <- glm(y ~ GMAT, data = admission, family = binomial)
summary(fit_gmat)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = y ~ GMAT, family = binomial, data = admission)
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) -17.397141   3.758928  -4.628 3.69e-06 ***
## GMAT          0.033609   0.007386   4.551 5.35e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 111.533  on 84  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance:  60.106  on 83  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 64.106
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
gmat_grid$prob <- predict(fit_gmat, newdata = gmat_grid, type = "response")

ggplot(admission, aes(x = GMAT, y = y)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = decision2), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(data = gmat_grid, aes(y = prob), colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(notadmit = "firebrick", admit = "steelblue")) +
  labs(x = "GMAT", y = "P(admit | GMAT)", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 5.1: Logistic regression of admission probability on GMAT

The slope is positive (\(\hat\beta_1 = 0.0336\)): a higher GMAT score increases the probability of admission. Each additional GMAT point multiplies the odds by \(e^{0.0336} \approx 1.0342\).

5.1 Predictions

predict(..., type = "response") returns probabilities directly.

p_ext <- predict(fit_gmat,
  newdata = data.frame(GMAT = c(min(admission$GMAT), max(admission$GMAT))),
  type = "response")
cat("P(admit | GMAT =", min(admission$GMAT), ") =",
    round(p_ext[1] * 100, 2), "%\n")
## P(admit | GMAT = 313 ) = 0.1 %
cat("P(admit | GMAT =", max(admission$GMAT), ") =",
    round(p_ext[2] * 100, 2), "%\n")
## P(admit | GMAT = 693 ) = 99.73 %

Predictions are now sensible probabilities, unlike the linear model above.

5.2 Verifying the formula by hand

It is instructive to verify that predict is indeed applying the formula. For a candidate with GMAT equal to the minimum value in the dataset, the fitted log-odds are \(\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 \times 313\), so the probability is:

b0 <- coef(fit_gmat)[1]
b1 <- coef(fit_gmat)[2]

for (gmat_val in c(min(admission$GMAT), max(admission$GMAT))) {
  log_odds <- b0 + b1 * gmat_val
  p_manual <- exp(log_odds) / (1 + exp(log_odds))
  cat("GMAT =", gmat_val,
      " | log-odds =", round(log_odds, 4),
      " | p =", round(p_manual * 100, 4), "%\n")
}
## GMAT = 313  | log-odds = -6.8775  | p = 0.103 %
## GMAT = 693  | log-odds = 5.8939  | p = 99.7251 %

These match the predict output exactly, confirming that the model is simply plugging the linear predictor into the sigmoid function.

6 Classification: the threshold

The logistic model outputs a probability, not a class. To obtain a class prediction we apply a threshold \(t\): predict admit if \(\hat p > t\), otherwise notadmit. The default \(t = 0.5\) is just a convention — the right threshold depends on the costs of each type of error.

6.1 The confusion matrix

For \(t = 0.5\):

admission$prob  <- predict(fit_gmat, type = "response")
admission$pred  <- factor(ifelse(admission$prob > 0.5, "admit", "notadmit"),
                          levels = c("notadmit", "admit"))

cm <- table(Real = admission$decision2, Predicted = admission$pred)
cm
##           Predicted
## Real       notadmit admit
##   notadmit       48     6
##   admit           6    25

The four cells of the confusion matrix are:

Predicted notadmit Predicted admit
Real notadmit True Negative (TN) False Positive (FP)
Real admit False Negative (FN) True Positive (TP)

From these we derive the main metrics:

  • Accuracy = (TP + TN) / \(n\) — overall fraction correct.
  • Sensitivity (TPR, Recall) = TP / (TP + FN) — fraction of real positives detected.
  • Specificity (TNR) = TN / (TN + FP) — fraction of real negatives correctly identified.
  • Precision (PPV) = TP / (TP + FP) — fraction of predicted positives that are real.
confusion_metrics <- function(cm) {
  TN <- cm[1,1]; FP <- cm[1,2]; FN <- cm[2,1]; TP <- cm[2,2]
  n  <- sum(cm)
  data.frame(
    Accuracy    = round((TP + TN) / n, 3),
    Sensitivity = round(TP / (TP + FN), 3),
    Specificity = round(TN / (TN + FP), 3),
    Precision   = round(TP / (TP + FP), 3)
  )
}

confusion_metrics(cm)

6.2 Changing the threshold

Raising the threshold makes the model more conservative: fewer admits are predicted, so sensitivity drops and specificity rises. Lowering it does the opposite.

thresholds <- seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.01)

metrics_df <- lapply(thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- factor(ifelse(admission$prob > t, "admit", "notadmit"),
                 levels = c("notadmit", "admit"))
  cm_t <- table(Real = admission$decision2, Predicted = pred)
  if (nrow(cm_t) < 2 || ncol(cm_t) < 2) return(NULL)
  TN <- cm_t[1,1]; FP <- cm_t[1,2]; FN <- cm_t[2,1]; TP <- cm_t[2,2]
  n  <- sum(cm_t)
  data.frame(threshold   = t,
             Accuracy    = (TP + TN) / n,
             Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN),
             Specificity = TN / (TN + FP))
}) %>% bind_rows()

metrics_df %>%
  pivot_longer(-threshold, names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = threshold, y = Value, colour = Metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Accuracy    = "steelblue",
                                 Sensitivity = "firebrick",
                                 Specificity = "darkorange")) +
  labs(x = "Threshold", y = "Metric value", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 6.1: Classification metrics as a function of the threshold

There is always a trade-off: you cannot simultaneously maximise sensitivity and specificity. The right choice depends on the problem — in medical screening you may prefer high sensitivity (miss fewer true cases) even at the cost of more false alarms.

7 The ROC curve and AUC

Instead of committing to one threshold, the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve traces the sensitivity–specificity trade-off across all possible thresholds. It is the standard way to evaluate a binary classifier independently of any particular threshold.

The Area Under the Curve (AUC) summarises the ROC in a single number: AUC = 1 is a perfect classifier, AUC = 0.5 is no better than random (the diagonal), and AUC < 0.5 means the model is systematically wrong.

roc_gmat <- roc(admission$y, admission$prob, quiet = TRUE)

ggroc(roc_gmat, colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(slope = -1, intercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.3, y = 0.2,
           label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc(roc_gmat), 3)), size = 4.5) +
  labs(x = "Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 7.1: ROC curve for the logistic model with GMAT only

Note: ggroc from pROC plots specificity on the x-axis decreasing from left to right, so the random-classifier diagonal runs from top-left to bottom-right.

7.1 Optimal threshold from the ROC curve

One data-driven way to pick a threshold is to find the point on the ROC curve closest to the top-left corner (perfect sensitivity and specificity), which minimises the combined error rate.

coords(roc_gmat, "best",
       ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"))

8 Two predictors: GPA and GMAT

Adding GPA alongside GMAT gives a more realistic model.

fit_two <- glm(y ~ GPA + GMAT, data = admission, family = binomial)
summary(fit_two)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = y ~ GPA + GMAT, family = binomial, data = admission)
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
## (Intercept) -223.6349   162.5991  -1.375    0.169
## GPA           41.2864    29.5065   1.399    0.162
## GMAT           0.1965     0.1473   1.334    0.182
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 111.5332  on 84  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance:   6.5035  on 82  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 12.504
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 12

8.1 Decision boundary

With two numeric predictors the decision boundary — the set of points where \(\hat p = 0.5\) — is a straight line in the predictor space. At threshold \(t = 0.5\) the logit equals 0, giving:

\[\text{GMAT} = -\frac{\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 \cdot \text{GPA}}{\hat\beta_2}\]

b <- coef(fit_two)
gpa_seq  <- seq(min(admission$GPA), max(admission$GPA), length.out = 200)
boundary <- data.frame(GPA  = gpa_seq,
                       GMAT = -(b[1] + b[2] * gpa_seq) / b[3])

ggplot(admission, aes(x = GPA, y = GMAT, colour = decision2)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_line(data = boundary, aes(x = GPA, y = GMAT),
            colour = "black", linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(notadmit = "firebrick", admit = "steelblue")) +
  labs(colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 8.1: Decision boundary of the two-predictor logistic model at threshold 0.5

8.2 Comparing the two models with ROC

admission$prob_two <- predict(fit_two, type = "response")
roc_two <- roc(admission$y, admission$prob_two, quiet = TRUE)

roc_list <- list(
  "GMAT only"  = roc_gmat,
  "GPA + GMAT" = roc_two
)

ggroc(roc_list, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = -1, intercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("GMAT only" = "steelblue", "GPA + GMAT" = "firebrick"),
    labels = c(paste0("GMAT only (AUC = ",  round(auc(roc_gmat), 3), ")"),
               paste0("GPA + GMAT (AUC = ", round(auc(roc_two),  3), ")"))
  ) +
  labs(x = "Specificity", y = "Sensitivity", colour = NULL) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Figure 8.2: ROC curves: GMAT only vs GPA + GMAT

Adding GPA increases the AUC from 0.907 to 0.999, a meaningful improvement.

9 Pseudo \(R^2\)

Unlike linear regression, logistic regression has no natural \(R^2\). The most common substitute is McFadden’s pseudo \(R^2\):

\[R^2_{\text{McFadden}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Residual deviance}}{\text{Null deviance}}\]

It equals 0 for a model with no predictors and approaches 1 as the fit becomes perfect. Values between 0.2 and 0.4 are generally considered good.

pseudo_r2 <- function(fit) 1 - fit$deviance / fit$null.deviance

data.frame(
  Model    = c("GMAT only", "GPA + GMAT"),
  Pseudo_R2 = round(c(pseudo_r2(fit_gmat), pseudo_r2(fit_two)), 4)
)

10 The probit model

The probit model replaces the logistic sigmoid with the normal CDF \(\Phi\):

\[p(X) = \Phi(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X)\]

In R only the link argument changes:

fit_probit <- glm(y ~ GMAT, data = admission, family = binomial(link = "probit"))
summary(fit_probit)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = y ~ GMAT, family = binomial(link = "probit"), data = admission)
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) -9.963885   1.927869  -5.168 2.36e-07 ***
## GMAT         0.019300   0.003818   5.055 4.29e-07 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 111.533  on 84  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance:  59.945  on 83  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 63.945
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
gmat_grid$prob_probit <- predict(fit_probit, newdata = gmat_grid, type = "response")

gmat_grid %>%
  pivot_longer(c(prob, prob_probit), names_to = "model", values_to = "p") %>%
  mutate(model = recode(model, prob = "Logit", prob_probit = "Probit")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = GMAT, y = p, colour = model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Logit = "steelblue", Probit = "firebrick")) +
  labs(x = "GMAT", y = "P(admit)", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 10.1: Logistic vs probit fitted curves — virtually identical in practice

The two curves are nearly indistinguishable. Logit is more common because \(e^{\hat\beta}\) has a direct interpretation as an odds ratio; probit is sometimes preferred in econometrics.

10.1 Verifying the probit formula by hand

Just as we did for the logit, we can verify that predict is applying \(\Phi(\hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 X)\) directly — using pnorm for the normal CDF:

g0 <- coef(fit_probit)[1]
g1 <- coef(fit_probit)[2]

for (gmat_val in c(min(admission$GMAT), max(admission$GMAT))) {
  p_manual <- pnorm(g0 + g1 * gmat_val)
  cat("GMAT =", gmat_val,
      " | p =", round(p_manual * 100, 4), "%\n")
}
## GMAT = 313  | p = 0.0044 %
## GMAT = 693  | p = 99.9676 %

The results match predict(..., type = "response"), confirming the formula.

11 Coding note: factor vs numeric response

A natural alternative to coding \(y\) as 0/1 is passing a factor directly to glm. This works, but R’s default factor ordering (alphabetical) may predict the wrong class.

# alphabetical ordering: "admit" < "notadmit", so R models P(notadmit)
contrasts(factor(admission$decision2))
##          admit
## notadmit     0
## admit        1

The signs of all coefficients are flipped relative to our 0/1 model. To fix this, set the reference level explicitly so that the model predicts \(P(\text{admit})\):

fit_factor <- glm(
  factor(decision2, levels = c("notadmit", "admit")) ~ GMAT,
  data = admission, family = binomial
)
all.equal(coef(fit_gmat), coef(fit_factor), check.names = FALSE)
## [1] TRUE

Always check contrasts() when using a factor response; a sign flip means you are predicting the wrong class.

12 Multinomial logistic regression

When the response has \(K > 2\) classes we use multinomial logistic regression, fitted with multinom() from the nnet package. One class is the reference and the model estimates \(K - 1\) sets of coefficients, each giving the log-odds of that class relative to the reference.

Here we use the original three-class response: admit, border, notadmit.

fit_multi <- multinom(decision ~ GPA + GMAT, data = admission)
summary(fit_multi)
## Call:
## multinom(formula = decision ~ GPA + GMAT, data = admission)
## 
## Coefficients:
##        (Intercept)      GPA       GMAT
## border   -134.1421 36.96973 0.07002442
## admit    -260.4073 60.70567 0.17800171
## 
## Std. Errors:
##        (Intercept)      GPA       GMAT
## border   0.5895009 1.619554 0.01084123
## admit    0.4168283 2.337048 0.01590936
## 
## Residual Deviance: 13.1286 
## AIC: 25.1286
pred_multi <- predict(fit_multi)
cm_multi   <- table(Real = admission$decision, Predicted = pred_multi)

as.data.frame(cm_multi) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Predicted, y = Real, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "steelblue") +
  labs(fill = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 12.1: Confusion matrix for the multinomial model 

cat("Overall accuracy:",
    round(sum(diag(cm_multi)) / sum(cm_multi), 3), "\n")
## Overall accuracy: 0.965

The three-class model fits very well. Most off-diagonal errors land on the adjacent border class, which is expected given the overlap visible in the exploratory plots.

13 Summary

Concept Function Notes
Logistic model glm(..., family = binomial) default link: logit
Probit model glm(..., family = binomial(link = "probit")) link: \(\Phi\)
Predicted probabilities predict(fit, type = "response") always specify type
Confusion matrix table(real, predicted) depends on chosen threshold
ROC / AUC roc(), auc() from pROC threshold-independent evaluation
Pseudo \(R^2\) 1 - deviance / null.deviance McFadden; 0.2–0.4 is good
Multinomial multinom() from nnet \(K-1\) sets of coefficients
Factor response set levels explicitly check contrasts() to avoid sign flip

Key takeaways: logistic regression outputs probabilities; converting to a class requires choosing a threshold, which is a real modelling decision. The ROC curve and AUC let you evaluate and compare models without committing to a single threshold.
